text: The indication for surgery was a case of haematomatous polyposis of the colon causing protein-losing enteropathy with severe hypoalbuminia and anisarca was built up nutritionally before taking her up for surgery.

The surgeon begins the colectomy by mobilizing the right side of the colon. Using the left hand instrument, the ileocecal junction is lifted, causing tenting of the ileocolic vessels. An incision is made on the right colonic mesentery, entering the loose areolar tissue between the colonic mesentery and the retroperitoneum. The avascular plane is developed using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection.

In the lower abdomen, the surgeon identifies the right ureter, gonadal vessels, and iliac vessels covered by retroperitoneal fat. As the dissection progresses cranially, the duodenum is visible medially and the gerota fascia laterally. The surgeon carefully avoids the retro-duodenal plane, staying anterior to the duodenum while dividing the mesenteric attachment to the duodenum and the head of the pancreas. Laterally, the white line separating the retroperitoneal gerota fascia from the colonic mesentery is identified and the dissection proceeds in that plane.

This is a very important advantage of doing the resection in a medial to lateral technique because sometimes if we do in the lateral to medial technique, there's always a risk that you may accidentally enter the plane behind the geratous fascia or proceed retrorheodinarily.

The surgeon separates the mesenteric attachment, revealing the complete C-loop of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas medially. The right colon mesentery above the iliocolic vessels is incised, and the iliocolic vessels are doubly clipped and divided after being separated from the peritoneum and cleared of surrounding lymph nodes.

If the colectomy is done for a right colon malignancy we go to the origin of the ileocolic vessels on the supramyocentric artery but in this case for polyphosis we can go a bit Likewise, if we are doing a total proctocolectomy with a plan for a J pouch, we don't ligate the iliocollic vessels at all and in fact take the colonic branches of the iliocollic artery close to the colon.

The surgeon shifts the camera port from the suprapubic to the umbilical port and applies downward traction on the right colon at the hepatic flexure. The duodenocolic ligament and anterior mesocolic attachment on the duodenum are divided, revealing the plane created by retroperitoneal tunneling.

The surgeon divides and separates the hepatic flexure attachments from the gerota's fascia. The transverse colon is further mobilized by dividing the gastrocolic omentum and entering the lesser sac. Care is taken to avoid injury to the right gastroepiploic vessels as they come into view during the medial to lateral division of the gastrocolic omentum.

The right part of the transverse colon is fully mobilized, with the gastro-epiploic vessels preserved. The complete C-loop of the duodenum and the pancreas are visible. Adhesions in the lesser sac are carefully divided near the stomach wall to prevent injury to the tented mesocolic vessels.

The surgeon completes the division of the gastrocolic omentum on the right side, opening the lesser sac. Attention is then directed to the infracolic compartment, where the iliocolic, middle colic, left colic, and right colic vessels are identified for ligation. The middle colic and right colic vessels are noted as particularly challenging due to their proximity to the superior mesenteric vein, necessitating careful handling to prevent avulsion and bleeding.

The branching pattern of the vessels here is extremely variable. You may or may not have a right colic. The middle colic may have a right and left branch or sometimes divide into three branches. It may divide very early or slightly higher up. When we are not operating for a transverse colon malignancy, as in this case, it is better not to try and go too close to the origin of the vessels to avoid any injury to the superior mesentery twain as discussed above.

The surgeon has created a plane cranial to the vessels during the takedown of the hepatic flexure, exposing the pancreas surface by dividing the duodenal colic ligament. The vessel transaction is performed higher up, away from the origin of the middle colic, requiring separate ligation of the right branch of the middle colic, the left branch of the middle colic, and the right colic. The root of the transverse colon mesentery is divided in an avascular area to access the left branch of the middle colic vessels within the lesser sac.

The surgeon carefully dissects and clears fibrofatty tissue and lymph nodes around the large colonic vessels to ensure a safe length for securing clips and transection. The right colic vessels and both branches of the middle colic are divided, followed by the division of loose areolar tissue, completing the separation of the right colon and hepatic flexure from the duodenal pancreatic complex.

The surgeon performs lateral to medial mobilization of the right colon by dividing its lateral peritoneal attachments and moving it medially to join the previously created medial plane. The root of the small bowel mesentery is also divided from its retroperitoneal attachment, allowing the small bowel to be freely mobilized for anastomosis.

The surgeon joins the medial and lateral planes of dissection to complete the mobilization of the right colon. The terminal ileal mesentery is freed from its retroperitoneal attachment, revealing the retroperitoneal bed with visible gerota's fascia and the C-loop of the duodenum.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral mobilization of the left colon by applying traction to the splenic flexure, tenting up the left colic vessels. The peritoneum above and below the left colic vessels is divided, allowing entry between the left colonic mesentery and the retroperitoneum. The white line separating the colonic mesentery and the geratous fascia is visible, with a clear color difference between the colonic mesenteric fat and the gyrator's fat.
During the mobilization of the left colon and splenic flexure, the surgeon carefully avoids injury to the marginal artery, which is crucial for maintaining the vascularity of the left colon.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, separating the genitals from the colonic mesentery. On the right side, the genitals and duodenum are separated, while on the left side, the tail of the pancreas and genitals are separated from the colonic mesentery. The dissection progresses until the bluish surface of the spleen becomes visible.

The surgeon progresses laterally along the retroperitoneal plane during the medial to lateral dissection, dividing the lateral peritoneal attachment of the left colon from the medial side. In the cranial aspect of the dissection, the distal body and tail of the pancreas are encountered as retroperitoneal structures.

During the left colon mobilization, the surgeon carefully avoids entering the retropancreatic plane to prevent injury to the splenic and renal veins. The mesocolic attachment on the anterior aspect of the pancreas is divided, allowing entry into the lesser sac anterior to the pancreas.

The surgeon detaches the mesocolic attachment from the anterior aspect of the pancreas, moving from the distal body towards the tail. The retroperitoneal attachment of the splenic flexure is divided while protecting the tail of the pancreas. The left colic vessels are exposed, doubly clipped, and ligated after the peritoneum and lymph nodal tissue are cleared.

The surgeon completes the medial mobilization of the left colon after dividing the left colic artery. The distal transverse colon is separated from its omental attachment, and the gastrocolic omentum is divided to mobilize the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure. The linocolic ligament is divided, and the dissection plane is joined with the retroperitoneal plane, completing the left colon mobilization.
The surgeon frees the remaining length of the transverse colon by dividing its omental attachment from the left side to the right side, completing the mobilization of the left colon.

The surgeon begins the initial mobilization of the rectum along the Heels plane, preserving the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The lateral peritoneum below the IMA is divided, and the loose areolar plane behind the IMA is entered. The dissection proceeds laterally in the total mesorectal excision (TME) Heels plane until the ureter is identified, then continues inferiorly to the mid rectum. During posterior rectal mobilization, the loose areolar tissue of the TME plane is divided at its superior extent to prevent tenting of the inferior hypogastric plexus nerves, leaving a layer of fibro fatty tissue to protect the nerves.

A gauze piece is placed behind the inferior mesenteric artery and anterior to the identified ureter. The surgeon frees the sigmoid colon by dividing its lateral peritoneal attachment, along with the upper rectum, completing its mobilization. The gauze serves as a guide to connect the dissection planes and ensure the procedure remains in the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the sigmoid colon by freeing it from its retroperitoneal attachment up to the already mobilized descending colon. The sigmoid mesentery is divided by individually ligating and dividing the sigmoid vessels arising from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), preserving the IMA supply to the rectum.

The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) are visible coursing inferiorly, with four sigmoid vessels arising perpendicularly towards the sigmoid colon. These vessels are individually dissected, doubly clipped, and divided, preserving the IMA. The dissection continues up to the colon wall at the proposed site of transection at the recto sigmoid junction.

An on-table colonoscopy is performed to visually confirm that the rectum below the proposed transection site is free of disease. The sigmoid colon and left colon have been fully mobilized up to the rectosigmoid junction, with the inferior mesenteric artery preserved.

The surgeon delivers the specimen through a suprapubic incision. Using a linear cutter stapler, the ileocecal junction and rectosigmoid junction are transected, followed by a side-to-side iliorectal anastomosis through the same incision. The surgical bed is inspected for the middle colic, left colic, and sigmoid vascular pedicles, ensuring hemostasis. A 24 French drain is placed into the pelvis.

The middle colic pedicles and the lower border of the pancreas. The post-operative recovery was uneventful. The histology revealed haematomatous polyposis of the colon with no evidence of any invasive malignancy.
